text.title: Bash Aliases for Solo Developer Productivity Core Rules
text.summary: Testable rules every application of `Bash Aliases for Solo Developer Productivity` must enforce. Each rule pairs a binary check with a sourced rationale.

rule.statement: Use simple aliases for flag additions and substitutions; use shell functions for anything taking parameters, conditionals, or multi-step operations. Aliases-with-logic are rejected.
rule.rationale: Aliases cannot accept positional parameters; trying to do so produces brittle code. Functions handle parameters natively.

rule.statement: Destructive commands MUST be aliased with safety flags: `rm -i`, `mv -i`, `cp -i`. Missing safety alias = REJECT.
rule.rationale: A single absent -i on `rm -rf` on prod has no undo. Interactive flag costs one keystroke for a meaningful safety net.

rule.statement: Aliases referencing modern tools (eza, bat, fd, rg) MUST be gated behind `command -v` existence checks. Ungated aliases fail on minimal systems.
rule.rationale: The same ~/.bash_aliases is shared across machines; missing tool throws hard error on every new shell.

rule.statement: Short aliases (g for git, dk for docker) MUST wire tab-completion via `complete -F`. Missing completion = REJECT for any aliased command that has its own completion table.
rule.rationale: Without completion wiring, `g sta<TAB>` does not complete to `status`; rewiring is one-time setup multiplying alias value.

rule.statement: Aliases MUST live in ~/.bash_aliases sourced by ~/.bashrc, NOT inline in ~/.bashrc. Mixed files are rejected.
rule.rationale: Mixed files are harder to sync via dotfiles, harder to reload, and impossible to lint as a coherent unit.

rule.statement: When preconditions in `Applies If` fail OR any item in `Skip If` triggers, do not run this methodology. Document the skip rationale in the artefact's parent log.
rule.rationale: Misapplied methodologies are worse than no methodology — they consume cycles and produce false confidence.
